summary: Gruber reviews the iPhone 6S and 6S Plus, arguing that Apple's S-year upgrades are consistently underrated because they don't look new, even though they deliver the most significant engineering advances. He details how nearly every component has been replaced or upgraded despite the identical exterior, highlights the A9 chip's remarkable performance that rivals a MacBook, explores 3D Touch as a genuinely useful new interaction paradigm, and praises the camera improvements while lamenting that optical image stabilization for video remains Plus-exclusive. He maintains that the tick-tock cycle lets Apple's engineering teams make deeper improvements precisely because the form factor is known years in advance.
key_points: S-model iPhones are systematically undervalued because they look identical to predecessors, but historically deliver the biggest platform changes (Touch ID, 64-bit, Siri)
The A9 chip in the iPhone 6S matches or beats the new MacBook in single-core Geekbench performance, and no Android phone beats even the two-year-old iPhone 5S in single-core tests
3D Touch introduces genuinely useful interactions — trackpad mode for text editing, peek-and-pop for email triage, and edge-swipe app switching — though it conflicts with long-press gestures
Knowing the form factor years ahead gives Apple's engineering teams a massive head start to improve materials, components, and internal architecture
The 16GB base storage tier remains indefensible, and Apple's refusal to upgrade it suggests a deliberate pricing strategy to push buyers toward 64GB models
money_quotes: I think that's a trap — a way to be fooled by your eyes.
New-number iPhones (4, 5, 6) are about showing off Apple's design prowess. The S models are about showing off Apple's engineering prowess.
They don't look new, but almost everything about them is new.
Apple is literally years ahead of the industry.
The new iPhone 6S beats the new MacBook in single-core performance on Geekbench. That's astounding.
Trackpad mode is a once-you've-used-it-you-can't-go-back addition to iOS.
For a typical iPhone user on a two-year upgrade cycle, I think the S years are the better phones, historically.
It's too easy to fill them up. I wish Apple had switched the baseline from 16 to 32 GB.
key_insight: The S-model iPhones are Apple's most impressive engineering achievements precisely because the fixed form factor gives internal teams years of lead time to make deep, invisible improvements to every component.
